video_title: Intelligence on the Edge: Liquid AI's Ramin Hasani on the Search for Device-Native Foundation Models
gist: Liquid AI CEO Ramin Hasani lays out the investment case for device-native (edge) foundation models: a ~$1T 'dark compute' TAM in phones/laptops/cars rivaling the data-center buildout, a proprietary hardware-in-the-loop architecture-search engine (AFMD) as the moat, and commercial proof points (Mercedes-Benz, Shopify, #5 US HuggingFace downloads on only ~1,000 GPUs). His core technical bet: attention still wins at the frontier, but gated-convolution/subquadratic hybrids dominate under edge constraints, and hardware makers must move up-stack to own an 'intelligence layer' or lose differentiation.

insights.claim: The global smartphone market is roughly $500B/year and the laptop market adds more, so there is on the order of $1 trillion of 'dark compute' in people's pockets and desks that on-device AI can monetize, a market only now being rivaled in size by the data-center buildout.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Frames edge-AI as a data-center-scale TAM independent of hyperscaler capex; a tailwind for on-device model and edge-silicon plays.
insights.quote: The global annual smartphone market is about $500 billion for all of the hundreds of billions that are going into the data center buildout.

insights.claim: Liquid AI is explicitly targeting 'device foundation models' for every processor outside the data center: phones, cars, wearables, robotics, manufacturing, IoT and laptops, aiming to be an intelligence layer across heterogeneous edge hardware.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Positions Liquid as a horizontal edge-inference layer rather than a frontier-model challenger, a different competitive lane from OpenAI/Anthropic.

insights.claim: Inference demand for frontier models threatens to price much of the world out of the frontier market, and privacy plus the desire to control one's own data create structural demand for local, on-device inference.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Cost and privacy are the demand drivers underwriting the edge-AI thesis; watch inference-cost trends as the swing factor.

insights.claim: Energy constraints make it inevitable that compute outside data centers gets enabled, because there is not enough power to host all intelligence centrally, forcing a tiered stack where cheap tasks run on small local models and only the hardest problems hit the cloud.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Ties edge-AI adoption to the power-availability bottleneck; energy scarcity is a bullish catalyst for efficient on-device models.

insights.claim: Liquid AI ranks #5 in the US by weekly Hugging Face downloads with over 1 million downloads per week, behind only Google, Meta, Microsoft and Nvidia, and has released more than 50 model instantiations used in enterprises.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Concrete adoption metric placing a startup alongside big-tech labs on distribution; a credibility signal for a private company likely raising/valuing.
insights.quote: The top top ones from the US side are Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Nvidia. And the fifth one is Liquidi.

insights.claim: Liquid AI signed what it calls a historic contract with Mercedes-Benz to power in-car voice/audio and visual interaction with a model that is only 600 megabytes and fits on the smallest processor in the car.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: A blue-chip automotive design win validates on-device deployment economics; watch for OEM in-cabin AI as an edge-model beachhead.
insights.quote: a contract we sign it's actually a historical one with Mercedes-Benz where our models like is going to be powering kind of the audio and also the visual kind of elements inside the car

insights.claim: Liquid AI's models are in production at Shopify improving click-through rates across recommendations, search, product catalog and multimodal understanding.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: A live enterprise revenue-relevant deployment (CTR uplift) beyond edge devices, showing data-center 'constrained use case' monetization too.

insights.claim: Liquid AI's core differentiator is AFMD (Automated Foundation Model Design), a meta-learning system that puts target hardware in the loop and uses an evolution strategy to optimize memory, latency and speed across a search space of roughly 50-100 operators with no sacrifice on quality.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: The IP moat is the search engine, not any single architecture; harder to replicate than a model and reusable across hardware targets.

insights.claim: Liquid found proxy metrics like perplexity misleading and now evaluates candidate architectures on ~100 real downstream benchmarks running on the actual customer target hardware, not public benchmarks.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Downstream-on-target-hardware evaluation is the defensible practice; a caution that benchmark-optimized models mislead in edge procurement.

insights.claim: Liquid's search yields a scaling-law 'gradient on architecture': smaller and specialized models benefit from added biases (gating, recurrence, convolution), while at scale the winning architecture is progressively more unstructured, which is why transformers dominate above roughly 100B-1T parameters.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Predicts exotic/subquadratic architectures win the edge while attention keeps the frontier; segments the market by scale.

insights.claim: The LFM2 architecture that won Liquid's CPU search is 70-80% simple double-gated 1D convolutions plus roughly 30% attention layers, with all hand-tuned gating tricks stripped away, delivering competitive quality at much lower compute and memory.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Concrete architecture recipe: cheap gated convolutions can replace most attention on CPUs, undercutting inference cost for edge use cases.

insights.claim: Liquid ran architecture scaling-law experiments from 10 million up to 72 billion parameters across hybrid designs to derive its operator-selection rules, an unusually broad empirical search for a startup.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Signals the depth of compute/research invested in the moat; the search data itself is proprietary edge.

insights.claim: Liquid AI claims to be the most efficient foundation-model company on the planet and reached #5 US Hugging Face downloads using only about 1,000 GPUs in-house, a fraction of frontier-lab compute.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Capital-efficiency angle for investors: distribution scale achieved without a hyperscaler-sized GPU bill lowers the burn/valuation math.
insights.quote: we are probably the most efficient foundation model company on the planet

insights.claim: Hasani argues architecture research's real payoff is efficiency, not capability, and that the largest labs are 'wiping out the compute off of the planet' to train and host models, making efficient inference the key to broad AI access.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Reframes the value of architecture work as a cost lever; supports a bull case for efficiency-focused inference/edge providers.
insights.quote: the larger labs are wiping out the compute off of the planet

insights.claim: Hasani contends frontier-lab architectures are still decided by a small group of researchers hand-tweaking components based on personal intuition, a broken process that systematic/automated search and recursive self-improvement will replace.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Bets that automated architecture design displaces artisanal lab R&D; a directional call on where model-design value accrues.

themes.theme: Silicon partnerships & the intelligence-layer land grab

insights.claim: Liquid AI co-designs with AMD, Qualcomm, Intel and ARM, informing their next-generation ASIC/silicon roadmaps and building custom foundation-model computational graphs tailored to specific NPUs whose IP is undisclosed.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Deep silicon-vendor integration is a distribution moat and a hedge against Nvidia; watch edge-silicon vendors partnering on model IP.

insights.claim: Hasani argues hardware makers must move up-stack to own an 'intelligence layer' like Nvidia's Nemotron, into which he says a couple of billion dollars was invested to lower enterprise buyers' barrier to entry and sell more Nvidia hardware.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Predicts chip vendors must become quasi-model companies to differentiate; a strategic gap AMD/Qualcomm/Intel must close or outsource.

insights.claim: Hasani claims software and an owned efficient intelligence layer can overcome raw hardware disadvantages (memory bandwidth), so among AI-PC vendors the winner on end-user token speed will be whoever owns the model layer on their silicon, not whoever has the best specs.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Suggests competitive advantage in AI PCs shifts from hardware benchmarks to model-software co-optimization; reshapes vendor selection.

insights.claim: Hasani predicts the edge-AI future is increasingly vertically integrated, with models bundled and heavily optimized for specific hardware and therefore less swappable and modular than today.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Implies lock-in and reduced model portability at the edge, favoring incumbents that bundle model + silicon.
insights.quote: why do you want to change the model?

insights.claim: Hasani says kernel-level optimization is now automated by agents ('there is no CUDA mode anymore') and that Nvidia, Google (replacing Chromebook with Android/'aluminium OS' laptops) and Meta (building its own devices) are all pushing processor makers toward owning the intelligence layer.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Signals erosion of CUDA's hand-tuned moat and a broad device-plus-model integration race among big-tech platforms.
insights.quote: there is no CUDA mode anymore

insights.claim: Hasani expects smart glasses to become a major hardware medium that could eventually replace laptops, once someone 'gets it right.'
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Speculative edge-form-factor call relevant to wearables/AR device and edge-silicon investors.

insights.claim: Attention still generalizes better than any known alternative and continues to dominate the frontier because it is unstructured and scales to trillions of parameters, so subquadratic architectures like Mamba, SSMs and gated convolutions only win in constrained or specialized regimes.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Tempers the 'attention is dead' narrative; the addressable market for alternative architectures is the edge, not the frontier.
insights.quote: attention is a extremely rich unbiased format of algorithms

insights.claim: Liquid's closed-form solution to liquid neural networks (Nature Machine Intelligence, November 2022) removed the need for numerical solvers and let the models scale from hundreds to potentially billions of neurons, though nested nonlinearity still limits parallelization to sequential/subquadratic scans.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: The technical breakthrough behind Liquid's founding thesis and VC interest; explains why nonlinear architectures remain compute-bound.

insights.claim: Hasani claims priority on input-dependent gating, saying Liquid's 'Liquid S4' paper introduced input-dependent SSMs about a year and a half before Mamba, framing the mechanism now central to Mamba as a 'liquid structure.'
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: An IP/priority claim over a core modern architecture idea; relevant to Liquid's defensibility and research credibility.

insights.claim: Different data domains demand different winning architectures: DNA/genomics (100B-element sequences, tiny vocabulary) favor pure convolutions/SSMs/linear attention with no attention; video favors diffusion; pure audio-to-audio favors recurrent networks; and microsecond-latency tasks favor tiny differential-equation models.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: A domain-to-architecture map that argues no single architecture wins all edge verticals, supporting a search-based (not one-model) go-to-market.

insights.claim: Liquid's founding scientific bench includes Jimmy Smith (inventor of the S5 state space model and parallel-scan for SSMs) and Stefano Massaroli and Michael Poli (designers of the Hyena convolutional hierarchy), alongside its own nonlinear liquid-network lineage.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Concentration of alternative-architecture research talent is a hiring/IP moat; a due-diligence positive for the company.

insights.claim: Liquid will soon launch a self-serve fine-tuning platform, hookable directly from a terminal/CLI, that produces production-grade specialized small models at cloud-level quality for between tens and low thousands of dollars, and can synthetically generate its own training data.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: A forthcoming product that could commoditize custom small-model creation and pull demand away from frontier API usage; watch the launch.
insights.quote: between tens of dollars to let's say like low thousands of dollars to actually get you to the cloud quality

insights.claim: Hasani frames the future personal-computing primitive as a fine-tuned local orchestrator/router model that decides between small specialized local models, PII-stripping models, and cloud models, calling that router 'the computer.'
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Positions the on-device router as the key control point and privacy filter, a strategic layer between users and cloud APIs.

insights.claim: Hasani concedes no off-the-shelf local model today, including LFM2-24B-A2B (a mixture-of-experts with 2B active of 24B total parameters), matches frontier models like Claude/Opus/Fable on agentic tool-calling without task-specific fine-tuning.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Honest capability gap: local models need customization to compete, which is precisely the demand Liquid's fine-tuning platform targets.

insights.claim: Hasani predicts current algorithms will not reach the human brain's intelligence-per-watt (roughly 20 watts) because human intelligence embeds a long evolutionary 'pre-training' and multiple in-context learning mechanisms, whereas next-token prediction yields only a 'vague gradient descent' in-context learner.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: A ceiling call on how far miniaturized intelligence can go with today's paradigm; tempers hype on phone-scale AGI.
insights.quote: human brain that actually performs computation with 20 watts of power

insights.claim: Hasani argues architecture is only one axis and that the next breakthroughs in intelligence will come more from new learning algorithms, objectives, data, memory and priors than from architecture, which mainly serves efficiency.
insights.speaker: guest, Liquid AI CEO
insights.so_what: Signals where a research-first company thinks value migrates; investors should weight objectives/data innovation over architecture novelty.
